choices.note: START I reduced, but did not eliminate, strategic systems. Complete elimination of ICBMs was not the treaty's objective. That elimination-language was discussed at Reykjavik (1986) but never codified.

choices.text: Was a Soviet-US bilateral treaty that excluded the other four Soviet republics with nuclear assets (Ukraine, Belarus, Kazakhstan, and the RSFSR within the USSR)
choices.note: START I was USSR-US at signing, and after the USSR's collapse Ukraine, Belarus, and Kazakhstan became parties through the May 1992 Lisbon Protocol, committing to transfer Soviet warheads on their territory to Russia. The treaty explicitly brought those four successor states into the obligations.

choices.text: Banned missile-defense deployment by both parties, superseding the 1972 ABM Treaty
choices.note: START I was an offensive-arms treaty. The ABM Treaty (1972) governed missile defense and remained separately in force until the US withdrew in 2002. START I did not supersede the ABM Treaty or ban missile defense.

questions.explanation: START I was signed in Moscow just three weeks before the August 1991 coup and five months before the USSR's formal dissolution. Its uncertain fate in the chaos that followed was one of the central questions Western diplomats pressed on the Russian and Ukrainian governments: the December 1991 Alma-Ata declarations committed CIS states to honor Soviet treaty obligations, and the May 1992 Lisbon Protocol multilateralized START I to include Ukraine, Belarus, and Kazakhstan, paving the way for the 1994 Budapest Memorandum's denuclearization commitments.

questions.prompt: Gorbachev's May 1989 visit to Beijing — the first Soviet-Chinese summit in thirty years — is historically notable in part because:

choices.text: It normalized Sino-Soviet relations after the 1960s-80s split but was overshadowed by the simultaneous Tiananmen Square protests, which the Chinese leadership had to manage partly out of view of visiting Soviet officials and international press
It produced a joint Sino-Soviet military-alliance treaty aimed at countering NATO's 1989 Brussels summit decisions
choices.note: No Sino-Soviet military alliance was signed; the visit was about normalizing relations and ending the border dispute, not a NATO counter-alliance. Beijing remained non-aligned between Moscow and Washington.

choices.text: Gorbachev announced, during the visit, the unilateral dissolution of the Warsaw Pact within six months
choices.note: The Warsaw Pact was dissolved only on 1 July 1991, two years later, and not by unilateral Soviet announcement in Beijing. Gorbachev's Beijing visit was about the Sino-Soviet bilateral relationship.

choices.text: Deng Xiaoping used the occasion to endorse Gorbachev's perestroika and urge parallel Chinese reforms
choices.note: Deng's explicit view was the opposite — he saw Gorbachev's simultaneous political-and-economic opening as a cautionary example. China's 1989 path was firmer political control combined with economic marketization; Deng treated Gorbachev's model as an anti-pattern.

questions.explanation: The Beijing visit (15-18 May 1989) was meant to cap thirty years of Sino-Soviet estrangement — restoring state and party relations after the Brezhnev-era border tensions and the 1969 Zhenbao Island clashes. Its timing was politically disastrous for Deng: protesters at Tiananmen Square used the presence of international press covering the summit to press their democracy demands. The crackdown came three weeks later (3-4 June 1989), and Gorbachev's Beijing visit is often cited as a foil to highlight how differently the two communist systems responded to reform pressure — Gorbachev toward political liberalization, Deng toward economic marketization paired with political repression.
sources: Archie Brown, The Rise and Fall of Communism

questions.prompt: Mikhail Gorbachev was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in October 1990. The Nobel Committee's citation emphasized:

choices.text: His leading role in the peaceful end of the Cold War — specifically arms-control agreements (INF), the non-intervention stance toward Eastern European 1989 transitions, and German reunification
His management of the Soviet transition to a multiparty democracy and a market economy, completed by 1990
choices.note: The 1990 citation emphasized international peace achievements (end of Cold War), not Soviet domestic transition — which was still contested and incomplete in October 1990. The prize was not a statement on the success of perestroika at home.

choices.text: His personal intervention to prevent Soviet military action in the Baltic states
choices.note: Soviet force was actually used in Vilnius (January 1991) and Riga (January 1991) after the prize was announced, complicating this narrative. The October 1990 citation was not about Baltic policy.

choices.text: His dissolution of the CPSU and voluntary transfer of power to Boris Yeltsin
choices.note: No such dissolution or transfer had occurred by October 1990. The CPSU ban in Russia came only in late August 1991 after the coup; Gorbachev remained General Secretary until late August 1991 and President of the USSR until 25 December 1991.

questions.explanation: The Nobel came at a particular inflection point: the INF Treaty was three years old and fully implemented, the Berlin Wall had fallen eleven months earlier, German reunification had been formalized three weeks before the prize announcement (3 October 1990), and CFE would be signed in Paris within a month. The committee framed Gorbachev's contribution as restraint — not intervening in Eastern Europe, accepting strategic arms reductions, allowing German reunification within a NATO framework. The prize was politically useful abroad but domestically mixed: Soviet audiences increasingly identified Gorbachev with external concessions rather than domestic improvements. Gorbachev could not travel to Oslo to accept the prize until June 1991 because of domestic political pressures.

questions.prompt: Mikhail Gorbachev's state visit to Tokyo on 16-19 April 1991 was historically notable because it:

choices.text: Was the first visit by any Russian or Soviet head of state to Japan, and produced a joint communiqué that — for the first time — named all four disputed South Kuril Islands (Iturup, Kunashir, Shikotan, Habomai) as the subject of an unresolved territorial dispute, while producing no resolution or major Japanese aid package
Concluded a formal peace treaty ending the Soviet-Japanese state of war that had persisted since 1945, paired with a Japanese $30 billion aid package to the USSR
choices.note: No peace treaty was signed. Russia and Japan remain without a post-WWII peace treaty as of this date, with the territorial dispute still the principal obstacle. No major aid package was agreed.

choices.text: Produced a Soviet transfer of the two smaller Kuril Islands (Shikotan and Habomai) to Japanese administration, implementing the 1956 Joint Declaration
choices.note: Gorbachev did not transfer territory. The 1956 Soviet-Japanese Joint Declaration had envisioned such a transfer after a peace treaty, but no peace treaty was concluded.

choices.text: Produced a joint Soviet-Japanese declaration pledging mutual nonrecognition of North Korea pending a Seoul-led reunification initiative
choices.note: No joint Korea-policy declaration was issued. The visit focused on bilateral territorial and economic questions.

questions.explanation: Gorbachev hoped Tokyo would unlock large-scale Japanese investment to support perestroika. Tokyo conditioned any major economic aid on progress on the 'Northern Territories' — Iturup, Kunashir, Shikotan, and Habomai, seized by Soviet forces in August-September 1945 as Japan surrendered. The April 1991 communiqué explicitly named all four islands as subject to negotiation, a small Soviet concession, but without territorial resolution the large aid flow Tokyo implicitly offered did not materialize. The USSR collapsed eight months later; the dispute has persisted under Russia, with subsequent near-agreements (e.g., under Yeltsin and again under Putin) also failing.
sources: Gilbert Rozman, Japan and Russia: The Tortuous Path to Normalization

questions.prompt: On 1 September 1983 a Soviet Su-15 interceptor shot down Korean Air Lines Flight 007 (a Boeing 747 en route from Anchorage to Seoul) over Sakhalin after it strayed into Soviet airspace. The political significance of the shootdown was:

choices.text: It killed 269 civilians including a sitting U.S. congressman, prompted the Reagan administration to declassify radio intercepts and publicly accuse the USSR of murdering civilians, and deepened the 'evil empire' framing during one of the Cold War's tensest periods
The Soviet Union promptly admitted the error, offered compensation to the victims' families, and conducted a joint investigation with ICAO — an early case of Soviet-Western civil-aviation cooperation
choices.note: The USSR initially denied shooting the aircraft down, then admitted it but insisted the flight was a deliberate U.S. intelligence provocation. No timely compensation or joint investigation occurred; the recovered black boxes were concealed by Soviet authorities until Boris Yeltsin released them to ICAO in 1992.

choices.text: It triggered the Soviet withdrawal from the 1979 SALT II talks and the immediate collapse of U.S.-Soviet arms control until the 1985 Geneva summit
choices.note: SALT II had been signed in 1979 (though unratified by the U.S. Senate). KAL 007 worsened the atmosphere but did not formally end arms-control talks; INF negotiations continued and were suspended for other reasons later that autumn.

choices.text: It led NATO to cancel the November 1983 Able Archer exercise, defusing Soviet fears of a preemptive NATO first strike
choices.note: Able Archer 83 proceeded as planned in November 1983 and itself became a Cold War near-miss when Soviet intelligence reportedly misread the exercise as cover for a real attack.

questions.explanation: Pilot Gennady Osipovich's Su-15 fired two missiles at the off-course Boeing 747 after the crew failed to respond to warning shots. Among the dead was Congressman Larry McDonald (D-GA). The incident came amid severe tension: KGB Operation RYAN watching for a feared NATO first strike, Pershing II deployment imminent, U.S.-Mujahideen activity in Afghanistan, and the gravely ill Yuri Andropov leading the USSR. For the Reagan administration, KAL 007 validated 'evil empire' rhetoric; for the Kremlin, it deepened paranoia. The black boxes, recovered secretly by Soviet divers, were handed to ICAO only in 1992 on Yeltsin's orders.
sources: Seymour Hersh, The Target Is Destroyed

questions.prompt: Gorbachev's October 1987 Murmansk speech proposed:

choices.text: A Nordic nuclear-weapons-free zone, Arctic-waters arms restraint, and Soviet-Western cooperation on Arctic resources and the environment — framed as 'transforming the Arctic into a zone of peace'
The unilateral withdrawal of all Soviet forces from Afghanistan within six months
choices.note: Afghanistan withdrawal was pursued through separate diplomatic tracks (the Geneva Accords of April 1988, completed February 1989). The Murmansk speech's focus was Arctic and Nordic, not Afghanistan.

choices.text: The formation of a USSR-China military alliance against NATO encirclement
choices.note: Gorbachev pursued Sino-Soviet normalization (culminating in the May 1989 Beijing visit) but did not propose a military alliance. Certainly not at Murmansk, which was about Arctic and Nordic cooperation.

choices.text: A Soviet offer to buy the Panama Canal Zone from the United States and Cuba's foreign debt restructuring
choices.note: No such offers were made at Murmansk or elsewhere. The Murmansk speech was Arctic-focused, part of Gorbachev's broader 'common European home' and 'new political thinking' vocabulary.

questions.explanation: The Murmansk speech on 1 October 1987 is often cited as the single clearest articulation of 'new political thinking' applied to a regional file. It called for a Nordic nuclear-weapon-free zone, restraints on naval activity in Arctic waters, scientific cooperation, environmental protection, and opening of the Northern Sea Route to international navigation. Some of its proposals — notably Arctic environmental cooperation — ultimately fed into the Arctic Council (founded 1996). The speech is also an early signal that the USSR under Gorbachev viewed its northern strategic frontier as a possible site of confidence-building rather than force-buildup, a posture consistent with later arms-control decisions.

questions.prompt: The Geneva Accords on Afghanistan — signed on 14 April 1988 by representatives of Afghanistan, Pakistan, the United States, and the USSR — did which of the following?

choices.text: Established a schedule for Soviet troop withdrawal (to be completed within nine months, by 15 February 1989) and committed Kabul and Islamabad to non-interference, but left the legitimacy of the Najibullah government unresolved and did not stop continued U.S. and Soviet arms flows to the Afghan sides
Established a UN-commanded international peacekeeping force to separate the mujahideen and Afghan Communist forces, deployed before Soviet withdrawal completed
choices.note: No peacekeeping force was deployed. UN observers (UNGOMAP) monitored the Soviet withdrawal and cross-border non-interference but did not separate combatants.

choices.text: Imposed a mutually binding halt on U.S. aid to the mujahideen and Soviet aid to the Najibullah government, enforced by a joint commission with authority over border crossings
choices.note: The U.S.-Soviet understanding operationalized 'positive symmetry' — both sides could continue supplying their clients. Arms flows continued from both sides until the 1992 fall of Najibullah.

choices.text: Required mujahideen commanders to recognize the Najibullah government in exchange for a Soviet payment of $2 billion and the transfer of former Soviet military bases in Afghanistan to Pakistani administration
choices.note: The mujahideen were not parties to the accords and did not recognize Najibullah. There were no compensation or base-transfer provisions; the accords were negotiated among the four governments, not the insurgency.

questions.explanation: The Accords were four linked instruments, mediated by UN envoy Diego Cordovez: bilateral Afghan-Pakistani non-interference; U.S.-Soviet guarantees of that non-interference; a refugee-return protocol; and the withdrawal timetable. Soviet withdrawal proceeded on schedule from 15 May 1988 through 15 February 1989 under General Boris Gromov. But because the Accords left Najibullah in place and did not bind the mujahideen, fighting continued — Najibullah held on until April 1992, when Soviet aid finally stopped. The Accords are often cited as a model (and cautionary tale) of 'decent-interval' negotiated withdrawal.
sources: Diego Cordovez and Selig Harrison, Out of Afghanistan

questions.prompt: Soviet recognition of the independence of Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia was formally completed by:

choices.text: A State Council resolution on 6 September 1991 — the first decision of the new post-coup all-union executive body, which included Gorbachev and the heads of the participating republics
A bilateral decree by Boris Yeltsin and the three Baltic republics on 24 August 1991, without involvement of Gorbachev or the USSR's institutions
choices.note: Russian recognition came quickly after the coup (Yeltsin recognized the Baltics in late August 1991), but formal Soviet recognition required an all-union act, which came with the 6 September State Council resolution.

choices.text: The 8 December 1991 Belovezha Accords, in which the three Baltic states joined with Russia, Ukraine, and Belarus to dissolve the USSR
choices.note: The Baltic states never signed Belovezha and refused to join the CIS. Their independence was already formally recognized by Moscow on 6 September 1991, months before Belovezha.

choices.text: The January 1992 Alma-Ata Protocol, which brought the Baltic states into the CIS as full members
choices.note: Alma-Ata was December 1991 (not January 1992), and the Baltic states refused CIS membership from the start — both at Alma-Ata and afterwards. They oriented explicitly toward Western integration.

questions.explanation: The State Council's 6 September 1991 recognition was symbolically enormous: it was the first sovereign act of the post-coup USSR executive, it was chaired by Gorbachev, it validated independence declarations the Baltic republics had been making since 1990 (Lithuania 11 March 1990; Latvia 4 May 1990 restoration; Estonia 20 August 1991), and it preceded the UN's admission of the three states on 17 September 1991. The Baltic case is distinctive among the Soviet successor states: the three republics rejected the CIS from the beginning, oriented toward Europe, and achieved NATO and EU membership in 2004 — a path politically foreclosed to Ukraine, Belarus, Moldova, and the Caucasus states.

questions.prompt: The six-month sequence August 1989-December 1989 saw five Eastern European communist regimes transition or collapse. Which correctly orders them by date of the regime-changing moment?

choices.text: Poland (Mazowiecki government, 24 August 1989) → Hungary (opening of fence and HSWP dissolution, September-October) → East Germany (wall falls, 9 November) → Czechoslovakia (Velvet Revolution, late November) → Romania (Ceauşescu executed, 25 December)
Romania (first, August) → East Germany (September) → Poland (October) → Czechoslovakia (November) → Hungary (December)
choices.note: This is wrong on all dates. Romania's transition was last (late December 1989), and Poland and Hungary were earliest. The listed order reverses the actual sequence.

choices.text: All five regimes transitioned simultaneously on 9 November 1989 when the Berlin Wall fell, triggering a single bloc-wide collapse in one week
choices.note: The five transitions were spread over several months, not simultaneous. Each had its own trigger and local pacing — Poland (summer), Hungary (early fall), East Germany (November), Czechoslovakia (late November), Romania (late December).

choices.text: East Germany (August, border fence cut) → Hungary (September, wall fall) → Romania (October) → Poland (November) → Czechoslovakia (December)
choices.note: Border-fence cutting was Hungary's action in May-September 1989 (Hungary cut the fence on its Austrian border from 2 May, and opened it for East German refugees on 11 September). The Berlin Wall was in East Germany, not Hungary. This ordering scrambles actors and dates.

questions.explanation: The sequence matters because each transition cued the next. Poland's Round Table (February-April 1989) produced partially-free elections on 4 June and the Mazowiecki government on 24 August — the first non-communist prime minister in Eastern Europe since 1948. Hungary's fence-opening (June cutting ceremony; full opening to Austrian border 11 September) allowed East German refugees through, draining the GDR and making Honecker's regime unsustainable. Schabowski's 9 November press conference sent the Berlin Wall down. Czechoslovakia's Velvet Revolution (17-29 November) was peaceful. Only Romania's transition (21-25 December 1989) was violent, ending in Ceauşescu's execution. The cascade was tightly chained, with Gorbachev's non-intervention stance (the Sinatra Doctrine) supplying the permissive condition.

questions.prompt: Russia's assumption of the USSR's permanent seat on the UN Security Council in December 1991 was accomplished through which mechanism?

choices.text: A letter of 24 December 1991 from Boris Yeltsin to the UN Secretary-General — endorsed by the other CIS states via the 21 December Alma-Ata Declaration — asserting that Russia was assuming USSR membership (including the permanent Security Council seat and veto) as the 'continuing state' rather than as a new member
A UN General Assembly vote in September 1992 admitting the Russian Federation as a new permanent member and simultaneously expelling the USSR
choices.note: No such General Assembly vote was needed. Russia assumed the seat as 'continuing state' of the USSR on 24-25 December 1991, without a GA admission vote. The other successor states (Ukraine, Belarus already were UN members; others became new members through admission).

choices.text: A Security Council resolution 777 assigning the USSR's seat to Russia, analogous to the later Serbia-Yugoslavia case
choices.note: Resolution 777 addressed the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia / SFRY case in 1992. The Russia case did not go through a similar Security Council contested-succession process; Russia's 'continuing state' claim was accepted by the other P5 without a vote.

choices.text: A unilateral Russian decree, rejected by France and the UK but acquiesced to after Clinton's inauguration in January 1993
choices.note: France, the UK, the US, and China all accepted Russia's claim as continuing state by late December 1991. No US-led 1993 intervention was necessary; the December 1991 letter was sufficient.

questions.explanation: The 'continuing state' framing was a deliberate legal choice with major consequences. It allowed Russia to inherit not just the UN Security Council seat but also the USSR's treaty obligations (including START I, CFE, INF), its embassies and diplomatic property abroad, and its external debt. The other thirteen (non-Baltic) successor states joined the UN separately as new members (Ukraine and Belarus retained their 1945 UN seats), accepted their share of Soviet assets through CIS agreements, and implicitly agreed to the asymmetric 'continuing state' status. This framing also preserved the Russian nuclear arsenal under a single command, a concern shared by Washington at the time.

questions.prompt: START II, signed in Moscow on 3 January 1993 by George H. W. Bush and Boris Yeltsin, would have done which of the following?

choices.text: Reduced US and Russian strategic warheads to 3,000-3,500 each and banned all MIRVed land-based ICBMs — a step that would have disproportionately constrained Russia's ICBM-heavy force posture relative to the US submarine-heavy posture
Reduced US-Russia strategic warheads to zero by 2003, effectively eliminating strategic nuclear weapons
choices.note: START II was a reduction treaty, not an abolition treaty. 3,000-3,500 warheads per side was the target, not zero. Complete elimination was occasionally discussed (Reykjavik 1986) but never codified.

choices.text: Brought Ukraine, Belarus, and Kazakhstan into the treaty as independent nuclear-weapon states, each retaining 1,000 warheads
choices.note: START II was US-Russia bilateral. Ukraine, Belarus, and Kazakhstan had agreed under the 1992 Lisbon Protocol to transfer their inherited Soviet warheads to Russia and join the NPT as non-nuclear-weapon states — the opposite of retaining independent nuclear arsenals.

choices.text: Established a joint US-Russia missile-defense system covering Europe and North America
choices.note: No such joint missile-defense system was established. START II was an offensive-arms-reduction treaty. Missile defense was governed separately under the 1972 ABM Treaty until US withdrawal in 2002.

questions.explanation: START II's MIRV-ban would have required Russia to either de-MIRV or decommission its heaviest ICBMs (such as the SS-18 'Satan') — a major asymmetric constraint since US strategic forces had shifted to submarine-based forces (which could still MIRV under START II). The Russian Duma resisted ratification throughout the 1990s; it finally ratified in April 2000 with conditions tied to US ABM Treaty compliance. When the US withdrew from the ABM Treaty in 2002, Russia declared itself released from START II, which therefore never entered into force. The follow-on SORT (Moscow Treaty, 2002) and eventually New START (2010) superseded it.

questions.prompt: Russia's deployment of peacekeepers to Bosnia in the mid-1990s under Western multinational leadership is best described how?

choices.text: A Russian airborne brigade joined IFOR (1996) and then SFOR — deployed under US operational command within NATO's Bosnia mission, a novel arrangement that made Russian troops subordinate in the field to US officers, with parallel reporting to Russian authorities in Moscow
Russia led a CIS-only peacekeeping operation in Bosnia, separate from IFOR/SFOR, under UN Chapter VII mandate
choices.note: No CIS-only Bosnia operation existed. Russian Bosnia peacekeeping ran through the NATO-led IFOR/SFOR structure, with the specific field-command-by-US-officer arrangement negotiated at US-Russia summits in late 1995.

choices.text: Russian troops were withdrawn from Bosnia in 1994 after the UNPROFOR experience and did not participate in subsequent IFOR or SFOR deployments
choices.note: Russia did participate in UNPROFOR (1992-95) and then in IFOR/SFOR (1996-2003). The Kosovo experience in 1999 was more contentious (Pristina airport dash), but Bosnia saw sustained Russian participation.

choices.text: Russia deployed to Bosnia exclusively in the Serbian-inhabited Republika Srpska and refused any command arrangement with NATO
choices.note: The Russian IFOR/SFOR brigade was deployed in the US sector (around Brčko), not exclusively in Republika Srpska, and operated under US operational command. The 'refused NATO command' framing describes neither the Bosnia arrangement nor the Russian position at the time.

questions.explanation: The Bosnia peacekeeping architecture was a Clinton-Yeltsin innovation. To let Russia participate without putting Russian troops formally under NATO command (politically impossible at home), Secretary of Defense Perry and Minister of Defense Grachev worked out a parallel reporting arrangement in late 1995: Russian airborne forces operating in the US sector received orders through a US officer who served dual-command-role (US and, through a Russian officer at SHAPE, Russia). This experiment in US-Russian cooperation was cited in the 1997 NATO-Russia Founding Act as a template for future consultation; the arrangement's collapse in Kosovo in 1999 is often cited as marking the end of the 1990s cooperation moment.

questions.prompt: The EU-Russia Partnership and Cooperation Agreement (PCA), signed at Corfu on 24 June 1994, did which of the following?

choices.text: Established the first treaty framework for EU-Russia political and economic relations — covering trade in most goods, political dialogue, and cooperation on rule of law — without granting Russia an 'Association Agreement' or any membership perspective
Offered Russia an EU Association Agreement comparable to those signed with Poland and Hungary
choices.note: Russia did not sign an Association Agreement with the EU; the PCA was a lower-tier framework that explicitly did not include the accession perspective that Association Agreements signaled for the Central European candidates.

choices.text: Made Russia a full member of the European Economic Area (EEA) with free movement of goods, services, capital, and people
choices.note: Russia never joined the EEA. The EEA includes EU member states plus Norway, Iceland, and Liechtenstein. The PCA was a bilateral cooperation agreement, not an EEA accession.

choices.text: Created a Russia-EU customs union that aligned Russian tariffs with the Common External Tariff
choices.note: No Russia-EU customs union exists. Russia later led the Eurasian Customs Union (2010) with Belarus and Kazakhstan, which was a rival integration project to EU engagement — the opposite of the framing here.

questions.explanation: The PCA was signed at Corfu at a 1994 EU summit that also initialed PCAs with other CIS states. Its ratification took longer than expected because of the First Chechen War (1994-96) complicating European approval; it entered into force only in December 1997. Substantively the PCA established most-favored-nation trade treatment (with exceptions), a 'Cooperation Council' political dialogue, and human-rights conditionalities. It is often cited as the high-water mark of 1990s Russia-EU convergence — before the 1999-2004 frictions over NATO and EU enlargements, Kosovo, and energy disputes diluted the framework into what became the 2003 'Four Common Spaces' architecture.
